articles.title: Hot Weather Trouble for Amazon Bugs
articles.summary: Scientists studied insects like moths and beetles to see how they handle hot weather. They found that many insects, especially in warm places like the Amazon rainforest, cannot easily get used to hotter temperatures. This is because their bodies have proteins that do not change quickly. If the Earth keeps getting warmer, up to half of the insects in the Amazon could face dangerous heat stress. Insects are important because they help plants grow by pollinating flowers and break down dead things. The study looked at over 2,000 insect species from places in Africa and South America. This research helps us understand why insects might struggle as the climate changes.

articles.title: Scientists Find a Special Protein That Malaria Parasites Need to Live
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered a special protein called ARK1 that malaria parasites cannot live without. Malaria is a serious disease caused by tiny parasites that spread through mosquito bites. These parasites grow and divide inside people and mosquitoes to make more parasites and spread the disease. The researchers found that ARK1 acts like a traffic controller, helping the parasites divide properly by organizing their genetic material. When scientists turned off ARK1 in lab tests, the parasites could not divide correctly and stopped growing. This means ARK1 is a key target for new medicines to fight malaria. The protein is different from similar ones in human cells, so drugs could target it without harming people. This discovery could lead to better treatments to stop malaria and save lives.

articles.title: A Super-Fast Light Detector That Works Like Magic
articles.summary: Scientists at Duke University made a new device that can see light super fast, in just 125 picoseconds, which is way quicker than a blink. This device is called a photodetector, and it works by feeling the tiny heat from light. It can see all colors of light, even ones our eyes can't see, and it doesn't need batteries or to be cold. It's very thin and can be put on tiny chips. This could help make better cameras for finding skin cancer, checking food, and helping farmers grow crops. The device uses special tiny cubes made of silver to trap light energy, making it work quickly and efficiently. This is a big step because older detectors were slow and bulky. The team shared their work in a science journal.

articles.title: Heart Medicine Helps After a Heart Attack
articles.summary: Some medicines that help people lose weight might also help the heart after a heart attack. A new study from scientists in the UK found that these drugs, called GLP-1 drugs, can make tiny blood vessels in the heart open up again. When someone has a heart attack, blood cannot reach all parts of the heart, which can cause more damage. The study shows that GLP-1 drugs relax small cells called pericytes, allowing blood to flow better. This could help prevent problems like 'no-reflow,' where blood stays blocked. The research was done on animals and might help doctors treat heart attack patients better in the future. These drugs are already used for diabetes and obesity, so they could be a safe option.

articles.title: TerraPower Starts Building a New Nuclear Plant in Wyoming
articles.summary: TerraPower, a company backed by Bill Gates, has received permission to start building its first nuclear power plant in Kemmerer, Wyoming. This is a big step because it's the first new nuclear construction approved in the US in almost ten years. The plant, called Natrium, is special because it uses liquid sodium for cooling instead of water, which makes it safer and more efficient. It also has energy storage, so it can save heat to make electricity later, helping it work well with renewable energy like solar and wind. The plant is smaller than most nuclear plants and is expected to be finished by 2030. Before it can operate, it still needs another license, but starting construction is an important milestone for clean energy.

articles.title: AI Learns About Genes from Many Living Things
articles.summary: Scientists made a smart computer program called Evo 2 that learns about genes from all types of living things, like bacteria, tiny archaea, and bigger plants and animals. It was trained on trillions of tiny parts of DNA called bases. This helps it find important spots in genes, like where genes start and stop or how they are controlled, which can be hard for people to see. The program is open source, meaning anyone can use it for free. It works by looking at patterns in DNA, similar to how our brains learn from lots of examples. This can help scientists understand genes better and discover new things about how living things work.
